meta.title: Bolt Torque Calculator | Professional Engineering Guide | AluCalc
meta.description: Master bolt torque calculation using the K-factor method. Professional guide on preloads, friction coefficients, and mechanical standards.

seo.h1: Bolted Joint Integrity: The Science of Torque and Preload
seo.intro: Developing a reliable bolted joint requires more than just tightening a nut. It necessitates a precise understanding of the relationship between applied torque and the resulting axial clamp force (preload). This guide provides the technical foundation for specifying torque values in industrial applications.
seo.practical: Essential for high-stress applications including engine cylinder heads, structural steel girders, and chemical pressure vessel flanges where joint separation can lead to catastrophic failure.
seo.example: To achieve a 45kN preload on an M12 (12mm) Grade 8.8 bolt with 'dry' surface conditions (K=0.20): T = 0.20 * 45,000 * 0.012 = 108 Nm.
seo.step_by_step:
1. Identify the required Clamp Force for the application.
2. Determine the Friction Profile (Nut Factor) of your hardware.
3. Verify the Bolt Class/Grade to ensure yield limits aren't exceeded.
4. Apply torque using a calibrated tool.

technicalSections.title: The Physics of Axial Preload
content: A bolted joint behaves as a spring system. When a bolt is tightened, it stretches elastically, creating a clamp force that holds the members together. This internal tension is called 'Preload'.

The primary goal of torque application is to achieve a preload that exceeds the maximum external service load. This prevents cyclic loading on the bolt, which is the leading cause of fatigue failure in machinery.

technicalSections.title: The Friction Dilemma: Nut Factor (K)
content: In a standard bolted assembly, approximately 90% of the input torque is consumed by friction—50% at the bolt head/nut face and 40% in the thread contact. Only 10% actually generates preload.
The 'Nut Factor' (K) is a simplified coefficient used to account for these variables. Using the wrong K-factor can lead to 50% errors in actual preload.

technicalSections.title: Mechanical Standards: ISO 898-1
content: Designers must ensure that the target preload does not exceed the Proof Load of the bolt material. Typically, structural joints are tightened to 75-90% of the material's yield strength.
